coredata.dc:title: Visual tracking of real-time freshness of fish using an agar hydrogel colorimetric indicator containing CuNPs/NCQDs

Shekarchizadeh, Hajar
coredata.dc:description: A simple, selective, and affordable dual fluorescence-colorimetric indicator for hydrogen sulfide was developed based on a complex of copper nanoparticles and N-doped carbon quantum dots (CuNPs/NCQDs). Real-time and visual freshness tracking of fish was done using a colorimetric indicator by incorporating CuNPs/NCQDs into agar hydrogel (AH-CuNPs/NCQDs). The fluorescence response of the CuNPs/NCQDs solution is quenched upon exposure to H2S. The field-emission scanning electron microscopy image of the AH-CuNPs/NCQDs film revealed a unified structure. The prepared indicator exhibited a good and irreversible response to H2S, with a LOD of 91.36 and a LOQ of 276.86 μM, based on the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) mechanism. The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer results confirmed the formation of a CuS bond in the colorimetric indicator exposed to fish spoilage. The prepared indicator demonstrated good stability and remained unaffected by pH or other volatile compounds. Notably, there was a strong correlation between ΔΕ and fish freshness parameters (pH, TV-BN, and TVC). Light green, pale yellow, and dark yellow colors, respectively, indicated freshness, semi-freshness, and spoilage of fish during storage in the refrigerator. Overall, the prepared indicator can be effectively used for detecting spoilage in meat products as a highly sensitive freshness indicator.

head.ce:title: Visual tracking of real-time freshness of fish using an agar hydrogel colorimetric indicator containing CuNPs/NCQDs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science and Technology, College of Agriculture, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science and Technology, College of Agriculture, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156-83111, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, selective, and affordable dual fluorescence-colorimetric indicator for hydrogen sulfide was developed based on a complex of copper nanoparticles and N-doped carbon quantum dots (CuNPs/NCQDs). Real-time and visual freshness tracking of fish was done using a colorimetric indicator by incorporating CuNPs/NCQDs into agar hydrogel (AH-CuNPs/NCQDs). The fluorescence response of the CuNPs/NCQDs solution is quenched upon exposure to H2S. The field-emission scanning electron microscopy image of the AH-CuNPs/NCQDs film revealed a unified structure. The prepared indicator exhibited a good and irreversible response to H2S, with a LOD of 91.36 and a LOQ of 276.86 μM, based on the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) mechanism. The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer results confirmed the formation of a CuS bond in the colorimetric indicator exposed to fish spoilage. The prepared indicator demonstrated good stability and remained unaffected by pH or other volatile compounds. Notably, there was a strong correlation between ΔΕ and fish freshness parameters (pH, TV-BN, and TVC). Light green, pale yellow, and dark yellow colors, respectively, indicated freshness, semi-freshness, and spoilage of fish during storage in the refrigerator. Overall, the prepared indicator can be effectively used for detecting spoilage in meat products as a highly sensitive freshness indicator.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since fish are rich in polyunsaturated fatty acids, essential amino acids, micronutrients, and vitamins, they have a higher perishability than other meat products (Luo et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2012). Fish spoilage is a complex process involving physical, chemical, and microbial mechanisms. Microbial activity is the main cause of fish spoilage through the accumulation of harmful substances (biogenic amines, volatile amines, sulfur compounds, and many volatile organic compounds) and unpleasant odors (Luo et al., 2022; Yu, Regenstein, et al., 2018).

Sulfide gases, including hydrogen sulfide (H2S), are one of the most important spoilage parameters of all types of meat (fish, pork, chicken, and sheep). The gradual breakdown of sulfur-containing amino acids during meat spoilage results in the production of these compounds. Microorganisms first convert sulfur amino acids into mercaptan and then into hydrogen sulfide. Abnormal levels of H2S are usually considered a key sign of meat spoilage. Therefore, it is very important to define the relationship between hydrogen sulfide values and other signs of freshness (Li, Li, et al., 2023; Lu et al., 2023).

Intelligent packaging is a new technology that integrates smart functions with the conventional packaging system. This packaging system can sense, detect, and record external or internal product changes to report information about food quality and safety (Shao et al., 2021). Intelligent packaging is divided into three categories: intelligent packaging containing indicators (integrity, freshness, and TTI), intelligent packaging carrying data (RFID and barcode), and sensors (gas, biological, chemical, electronic, and electronic nose and tongue sensors) (Akjouj & Mir, 2020; Luo et al., 2022). Freshness indicators control metabolites such as CO2, O2, volatile nitrogen compounds (NH3, TMA, and DMA), and hydrogen sulfide as an indicator of food freshness (Lee et al., 2019). The colorimetric freshness indicator directly reflects food freshness through color change and offers advantages such as simplicity, portability, low cost, real-time detection, and being non-destructive (Shao et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2022).

Recently, metal nanoparticles have been considered a substitute for colors in colorimetric indicators. High sensitivity and selectivity are the characteristics of metal nanoparticles (Kwon & Ko, 2022). Among metal nanoparticles, noble metal nanoparticles (Cu, Ag, and Au) have received much attention due to their special properties and localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR). Today, the use of gold and silver nanoparticles is limited, while copper nanoparticles are of great interest due to their low price, the presence of a localized plasmon band in the visible part, and low optical losses (Hatamie et al., 2014; He et al., 2022; Katyal, 2021). The mechanism of metal nanoparticles in freshness indicators is their color changes when the chemical reaction with special compounds produced during food spoilage is carried out on their surface (Kwon & Ko, 2022). Different studies have been conducted to detect sulfur compounds with nanometal indicators using different substrates, including silver nanoparticles on gellan gum, silver nanorods on cellulose paper, and silver‑iron nanotriangles on agar gum (Ahn et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2022; Zhai et al., 2019). Li et al. (2021) investigated an indicator using ruthenium nanoparticles to evaluate the freshness of pork meat based on the amount of hydrogen sulfide gas production. The results showed that with the increase in hydrogen sulfide gas concentration, the color of the ruthenium nanoparticle solution changed from orange to pink. Dodange et al. (2024) designed smart-active packaging using copper nanoparticles. Their colorimetric indicator, which contains copper nanoparticles, undergoes a color change from white to brown upon contact with hydrogen sulfide gas resulting from fish spoilage.

One way to improve the color change of metal nanoparticles is to complex them with carbon-based nanoparticles. Carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are quasi-spherical, zero-dimensional nanoparticles with a size of <10 nm. In recent years, they have attracted a lot of attention due to their new and unique properties that are closely related to structural defects and quantum confinement effects. Also, doping O or N heteroatoms on the surface of CQDs adjusts their intrinsic properties. Due to the abundance of functional groups, high conductivity, and optical stability, CQDs are very suitable for use in indicators together with noble metals to improve electronic properties and enhance LSPR (Horo et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2019). Zhuo et al. (2018) synthesized a Cu-CQDs fluorescent sensor for cellular H2S imaging in the living cell. The CQDs served as the fluorophore, and copper was responsive to hydrogen sulfide gas in this probe.

This study aimed to design a colorimetric freshness indicator based on an agar hydrogel containing copper nanoparticles/N-doped CQDs (CuNPs/NCQDs) complex to monitor fish spoilage during storage. According to previous research, copper nanoparticles can detect hydrogen sulfide gas. In this study, we demonstrated that the addition of N-doped carbon quantum dots enhances the sensitivity of copper nanoparticles. The CuNPs/NCQDs can detect H2S gas with clear and rapid color change using both fluorimetric and colorimetric methods through the LSPR mechanism. No previous study has investigated the identification of hydrogen sulfide gas released during fish spoilage using a complex of copper nanoparticles and N-doped CQDs within a hydrogel agar bed. The immobilization of CuNPs/NCQDs inside agar hydrogel can preserve the properties of CuNPs/NCQDs in detecting H2S gas, making them applicable in smart food packaging with improved color changes and a lower detection limit. Additionally, the incorporation of NCQDs alongside CuNPs enhances the moisture resistance of the agar hydrogel due to the hydrophobicity of NCQDs.

Fresh trout fillet was sourced locally (Isfahan, Iran). Agar powder (CAS:9002-18-0, Molecular weight = 336.33 g/mol, gel strength = 550–950 g/cm2, sulfated ash (600 °C) ≤ 6 %, 3,6-anhydrogalactose units content = 32 %, galactose content = 30 %, glucose content = 9 %.), plate count agar medium, and nutrient agar medium were purchased from Quelab Laboratory (Quebec, Canada). Chemicals such as copper (II) nitrate, sodium hydroxide, hydrazine hydrate, and others were purchased from Merck KGaA Co., Ltd. (Darmstadt, Germany).

The synthesis of Cu/NCQDs was done using the hydrothermal method according to the method reported by Wu et al. (2019) and Xiaomin et al. (2005). Firstly, an NCQDs solution was synthesized as follows: 0.945 g of PEI as a surface modification agent and nitrogen source was mixed with 0.1 g of citric acid and 30 ml of deionized water and stirred for 60 min. Then, 14 ml of 0.1 M copper (II) nitrate solution, 5 ml of 0.005 M sodium hydroxide solution, and 2 ml of hydrazine hydrate as a reducing agent were added to the NCQDs solution, and the mixture was transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 20 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, zeta potential (ζ), and crystalline structure
ce:section.ce:para: A transmission electron microscope (TEM) (CM 120, Philips, Netherlands) was used to investigate the morphology of the CuNPs/NCQDs complex before and after exposure to hydrogen sulfide (1 M). Particle size analysis was done using Image J software. The surface charge of the CuNPs/NCQDs complex was determined using a zeta sizer (ZEN3600, Horiba, Germany) before and after exposure to H2S gas (1 M) at room temperature. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the CuNPs/NCQDs complex was recorded using an X-ray diffraction spectrometer (ASENWARE, AW-XDM300, China) at 10 to 80° with a scanning speed of 1°/min and a step size of 0.02.

A fluorometric device (SHIMADZU, RF 5000, Japan) was used to investigate the excitation-emission behavior of CuNPs/NCQDs complex in the excitation wavelength range of 200–700 nm before (I1) and after (I2) exposure to H2S gas (1 M). The fluorescence intensity ratio (Ir) was calculated using Eq. (1) (Wen et al., 2019).

where ØST and ØX are the quantum yields of standard and CuNPs/NCQDs; IST and IX are integrated emission intensity fluorescence of standard and CuNPs/NCQDs; ASt and Ax are absorptions of standard and CuNPs/NCQDs; hST and hX are refractive index of standard and CuNPs/NCQDs (Zhou et al., 2019).

A UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Unico 2100, USA) was used to record the absorption spectrum of the CuNPs/NCQDs complex in the wavelength range of 200–700 nm before and after exposure to H2S gas (1 M). Also, the sensitivity parameter (S) of CuNPs/NCQDs to H2S gas was calculated using Eq. (3).
(3)
S = ∆ λ / ∆ h
where Δλ is the maximum absorption difference, and Δh is the refractive index difference of CuNPs/NCQDs before and after exposure to H2S (Afsari & Sarraf, 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CuNPs/NCQDs at different pH
ce:section.ce:para: Britton-Robinson buffer was used in the pH range of 5–11 to investigate the effect of pH changes on the CuNPs/NCQDs complex. 0.1 ml of the sample was added to 10 ml of Britton-Robinson buffer, and its absorption spectrum was recorded in the 200–700 nm range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of agar hydrogel colorimetric indicator based on CuNPs/NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.2 g of agar powder was mixed with 10 ml of distilled water at 100 °C for 1 h. Then, 10 ml of CuNPs/NCQDs complex was added and stirred for 2 min. The solution was poured into a Petri dish and dried at room temperature for 24 h (Yu et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of agar hydrogel colorimetric indicator based on CuNPs/NCQDs

ce:section.ce:para: The surface and cross-section of agar hydrogel colorimetric indicator based on CuNPs/NCQDs (AH-CuNPs/NCQDs) were analyzed using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) (MIRRA II, French).

The color changes of the AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator in the pH range of 5–11 was evaluated by immersing the indicators in Britton-Robinson buffer. Finally, changes in colorimetric parameters (L*, a*, b*) were investigated by a colorimeter (Nippon Denshoku, ZE6000, Japan). The ΔΕ parameter was calculated using Eq. (4).
(4)

where L0*, a0*, and b0* show the color parameters of the initial indicator, and L*, a*, and b* are the color parameters of the indicator after contact with various pH values.

Circular AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicators (2 cm diameter) were placed inside the lid of the glass vial containing H2S gas with different concentrations (0.5, 1, 10, 50, and 100 μM), and the changes in color parameters were determined to draw the calibration curve. Hydrogen sulfide gas was produced by adding Na2S to HCl so that the concentration of HCl was three times that of Na2S. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated according to Eqs. (5) and (6), respectively.
(5)
LOD =
3.3 σ
S
(6)

S
where, σ is the standard deviation, and S is the slope of the fitted line of the calibration curve (Teymouri & Shekarchizadeh, 2022).

A Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Jasco, FT\IR6300, Japan) was used to determine the FTIR spectrum of the AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicators before and after exposure to fish spoilage. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to evaluate the bond structure and valence layer of elements in the AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicators before and after exposure to fish spoilage by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy device (Specs, Slexps, Germany). Origin Pro software was used to analyze the results.

The specific response of the AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator to H2S gas was investigated. The AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator was placed in the lid of the glass vial containing volatile compounds such as ethanol, acetone, ammonia, trimethylamine, and acetic acid, and their color changes were checked (Zhai et al., 2019). To evaluate how the indicator responds to H2S gas in the presence of other volatile compounds, a fixed concentration of 100 μM H2S and different concentrations (5, 10, 50, and 100 μM) of ammonia, acetic acid, ethanol, and acetone were used simultaneously, and the colorimetric parameters were determined (Zhai et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fish freshness monitoring using AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh trout fillet (30 g) was placed in a sterile glass Petri dish. The circular AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator was placed in the lid of the Petri dish. The containers were sealed entirely with cellophane and put in the refrigerator (4 °C) for 10 days. The colorimetric parameters of the indicator and the quality parameters of the fish were determined during the storage.

10 g of fish was mixed with 90 ml of distilled water daily and then wholly homogenized to measure the pH of the trout fillet during 10 days of storage. Finally, the pH of the fish was evaluated by a pH meter (JENWAY, 3330, England) (Mohammadalinejhad et al., 2020). Total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) content was determined using the Kjeldahl distillation method according to Li, Liu, et al. (2023) method with some modifications. 10 g sample was added to 100 ml of distilled water and 2 g of magnesium oxide. Then, it was transferred to the automatic Kjeldahl nitrogen analyzer, and the resulting solution was titrated with 0.1 M sulfuric acid. TVB-N content was reported as mg N/100 g (Li, Liu, et al., 2023). The total viable count (TVC) was determined using the pour plate method. 10 g fish was combined with 90 ml of normal saline (90 %) and then entirely homogenized by Stomacher. Then, decimal dilutions were prepared, and finally, 100 μl of each dilution were cultured on plate count agar and incubated at 37 °C for 48 h. Finally, TVC was reported as the number of colony units per gram of trout fillet (CFU/g) (Yu, Zhang, et al., 2018).

An analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by a Fisher's test (LSD) at a significant level of p < 0.05 was performed using the statistical software SAS v.9. All tests were carried out in triplicate, and the data were reported as the mean ± standard deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, zeta potential (ζ), and crystalline structure
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1a shows the TEM image of the CuNPs/NCQDs complex. The particles are spherical, with an average diameter of 6.37 ± 1.6 nm. The particle size distribution diagram (Fig. 1b) shows that most of the particles are in the 5–6 nm size range. This shows that the hydrothermal method and CQDs as stabilizers could produce small and uniform nanoparticles. Zhuo et al. (2018) reported the average diameter of the Cu-CQDs complex to be 2–6 nm. The size of the CuNPs/NCQDs nanoparticles increased, the distance between them decreased, and aggregation took place when the CuNPs/NCQDs complex was exposed to H2S gas (Fig. 1C). Copper nanoparticles tend to interact with free carboxylate and amino groups through electrostatic interaction, which causes nanoparticles aggregation (Beiraghi & Najibi-Gehraz, 2017).

ζ potential of CuNPs/NCQDs nanoparticles was −0.26 ± 0.6 mV (Fig. 1d). The negative ζ potential is due to the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CQDs, but its negative values are low due to the presence of amine groups on the surface (Travlou et al., 2018). The ζ potential significantly increased to 9.8 ± 1.01 mV (Fig. 1e) when the CuNPs/NCQDs nanoparticles were exposed to H2S gas. Sulfide or thiol species readily release protons, which cause dipole-dipole coupling of electrons between the nanoparticles, stability decrease, and nanoparticles accumulation (Zhang et al., 2015). These results were similar to the results of Wang et al. (2021). They used the core-shell structure of gold and silver with a zeta potential of −12.3 mV to detect H2S gas (Wang et al., 2021).

XRD pattern of the CuNPs/NCQDs complex showed a broad peak at 23.5° (Fig. 1f), confirming the amorphous structure of CuNPs/NCQDs because the hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid groups strongly influenced the distance between the layers. On the other hand, the peaks of copper were not observed, which may be due to the low concentration of copper in the complex (Bano et al., 2020; Kazemifard et al., 2020; Zhuo et al., 2018).

Fig. 2a showed that the CuNPs/NCQDs complex had an excitation-dependent and another excitation-independent peak. The first peak is common in carbon dots and depends on different emission centers, emission peaks, and the surface state of the carbon dot (Aziz et al., 2019). Pretest results showed that the CuNPs/NCQDs complex had an excitation-independent peak at 500 nm with Ex = 450 nm. At this wavelength, the CuNPs/NCQDs complex solution had blue fluorescence (Fig. 2a). The CuNPs/NCQDs complex solution turned brown when it was exposed to H2S gas, and the peak was extinguished independent of excitation due to the formation of copper sulfide. The structure of CuNPs was gradually destroyed and turned into Cu2S/CuS by adding sulfide ions to the nanoparticle solution. This state is related to the internal filter effect (IFE) (Shojaeifard et al., 2019; Wen et al., 2019).

The fluorescence intensity ratio of the CuNPs/NCQDs complex was 1.825, which decreased to 1.038 after exposure to H2S gas. This shows that the nanoparticle solution was selective to H2S gas. Also, the quantum yield of the CuNPs/NCQDs complex was 50 % (or 0.50). Results showed that acetic acid, PEI, and copper nitrate can produce CuNPs/NCQDs nanoparticles with high quantum yields.

The results of the absorption spectrum of the CuNPs/NCQDs complex showed that this solution had a maximum absorption at a wavelength of 369 nm (Fig. 2b), confirming the spherical shape of the nanoparticles. There is only one absorption peak in spherical nanoparticles due to the existence of only one way to separate the charges inside the nanoparticle (Alberti et al., 2021). When the CuNPs/NCQDs were exposed to H2S, the maximum absorption peak shifted to higher wavelengths (411 nm) in the visible range. So, a significant red shift occurred corresponding to the Π-Π* transition. In this study, the solution turned from light green to brown due to the formation of CuS and Cu2S (Shojaeifard et al., 2019; Yu, Zhang, et al., 2018). Properties such as size, morphology, and refractive index affect the position of the LSPR peak. In fact, due to the binding of the analyte, biological or chemical connection, the dielectric properties of the environment and the refractive index will change, which causes the LSPR peak shift to higher or lower wavelength (Mohammadalinejhad et al., 2020; Rodriguez et al., 2020). Therefore, when H2S was attached to the nanoparticles, their size grew, and the distance between them decreased because of changes in the refractive index and the dielectric properties of the environment. This led to a red shift and increased the intensity of the LSPR peak. These results were consistent with TEM images. In addition, the sensitivity parameter of the Cu-NCQDs complex was 2.1 × 104
nm / RIU
when the nanoparticles were exposed to H2S gas. Sachdev et al. (2016) reported that due to the contact of H2S gas from onion spoilage with silver nanoparticles, the peak of LSPR was shifted from 420 nm to 547 nm (Sachdev et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CuNPs/NCQDs at different pHs
ce:section.ce:para: Results showed that different pHs did not make a noticeable difference in the color of the solution. No effect of different pHs on the LSPR peak was also observed by spectrophotometer, which indicated the lack of accumulation of CuNPs/NCQDs nanoparticles at different pHs (Bankoti et al., 2017). Fig. 2c shows that the absorption spectrum of CuNPs/NCQDs complex had two peaks at different pHs. One peak was around 230 nm, and the other was around 360 nm. The first peak is probably related to the Britton-Robinson buffer, and the second peak is related to the sample, which was consistent with the LSPR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of agar hydrogel colorimetric indicator based on CuNPs/NCQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows FE-SEM images of the surface and cross-section of agar hydrogel containing CuNPs/NCQDs. The AH-CuNPs/NCQDs had a smooth surface with small holes, showing that the nanoparticles were evenly spread on the surface of the film. The small holes may be caused by gas escaping while the film is drying. However, these holes can increase the surface and ultimately increase the sensitivity to H2S gas (Zhang et al., 2023). The cross-sectional image showed a relatively good continuity without cracks and porosity in the film matrix.

Colorimetric parameters of AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicators at different pHs are shown in Fig. 3c. No significant change in the colorimetric parameters of the indicators was observed at a pH range of 5–11 (p ≤ 0.05). These results confirmed the stability of the indicator containing CuNPs/NCQDs nanoparticles in a wide range of pHs.

Results showed that different concentrations of H2S caused a significant difference (p ≤ 0.05) in the colorimetric parameters of the AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator (Table 2 ). The brightness parameter (L*) decreased, and the yellowness (b*) and redness (a*) parameters increased with the increase in H2S gas concentration. Hence, the color of the indicators gradually turned light yellow and finally dark yellow. In fact, a strong interaction between copper nanoparticles and H2S formed due to the functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen on the skeleton of CuNPs/NCQDs. As a result, due to the formation of CuS or Cu2S, the accumulation of nanoparticles and color change of the AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator occured (Beiraghi & Najibi-Gehraz, 2017; Shojaeifard et al., 2019).

Fig. 3d showed a good correlation between ΔΕ of AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator and concentration of H2S (R2 = 0.9724). So, the lowest limit of detection (LOD) and the lowest limit of measurement (LOQ) were 91.36 and 276.86 μM, respectively (Linear range = 0.5–100 μM, σ =3.9758, S = 0.1436). Teymouri and Shekarchizadeh (2022) reported that the LOD and LOQ parameters of paper indicator containing copper nanoparticles were 1.53 and 4.63 μg / m l (101.77 and 308.41 μM), respectively. The better detection ability of AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator can be related to the following reasons: 1) Using agar polymer as a stabilizing substrate; agar hydrogel consists of organic polysaccharides with a lot of water, which causes sulfide absorption in the indicator (Yu et al., 2022), 2) Using CQDs; these compounds can intensify LSPR due to their functional groups, and on the other hand, doping nitrogen on the CQDS surface improves the interaction between the target analyte and CQDs due to the increase in the surface charge of CQDs (Lan et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2019).

Fig. 4 shows the FTIR spectra of AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator before and after exposure to fish spoilage. The absorption band at 3400 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of -OH in carboxyl groups due to the presence of H2O after the hydrothermal reaction (Liu et al., 2020). The symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of the CH band was observed at the wavelengths of 2923 cm−1 and 2845 cm−1, respectively (Moradi et al., 2019. (The absorption band of 1638 cm−1 and 1072 cm−1 is related to the NH and CN stretching band, respectively. The intensity increase of this band when exposed to fish spoilage is due to the increase of volatile nitrogen compounds during fish spoilage. The absorption bands around 890 cm−1 and 580 cm−1 are related to Cu-O-H and CuO, respectively. When the indicator was exposed to fish spoilage, Cu-O-H increased and CuO decreased, respectively (Das et al., 2020; Jayeoye & Rujiralai, 2020; Raul et al., 2014; Razaghianpour et al., 2022). The main absorption band was the stretching vibrational bond of CuS in the wavenumber of 1380 cm−1, which increased after exposure to fish spoilage. This shows that copper nanoparticles have been able to bond with H2S produced during fish spoilage (Wu et al., 2012). This band is present in the prepared indicator because the agar polymer contains sulfuric esters in the galactan (Mahmoud Nasef et al., 2011).

Fig. 5 shows the overall XPS spectra of AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator before and after exposure to fish spoilage. Three main peaks are observed in the XPS spectra (A1 and A2). The binding energies of 284.55, 399.3, and 532 eV correspond to C1S, N1S, and O1S, respectively. Also, the presence of copper and sulfur elements can be seen in the XPS spectra. The high-resolution spectrum of O1S (B1) has a high-intensity peak at the binding energy of 532 eV, related to the C-OH/C-O-C bond. The intensity of this peak increased after exposure to fish spoilage (B2), which can be due to exposure to oxygen and moisture (Zhang et al., 2017). The high-resolution spectrum of N1S with a binding energy of 399.3 eV is shown in Fig. 5C1, which is related to the NC bond (Luo et al., 2024). The increase in this peak intensity when the indicator was exposed to fish spoilage (C2) is probably due to the formation of bonds with volatile nitrogen compounds because nitrogen compounds are produced during fish spoilage as microbial metabolites due to the growth and activity of microorganisms (Connolly, 2007). The high-resolution spectrum of C1S with the binding energy at 284.55 eV corresponds to the C-C/C=C bond (D1). The intensity of this peak decreased after exposure to fish spoilage (D2), indicating that part of this bond reacted with the H2S gas produced during spoilage (Tammina & Rhim, 2023). Fig. 5E1 shows the high-resolution spectrum of S2P. The presence of the S2P in the XPS spectrum of AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator is due to sulfur groups in the agar polymer, as confirmed by the FTIR results. This spectrum is divided into four separate peaks at binding energies of 160.55, 164.15, 167.15, and 170.15 eV corresponding to CuS (S2p3/2), CS (S2p3/2), SO (S2p3/2), and SO4 2− (sulfate), respectively, according to the NIST database. The increase in the intensity of these peaks in the indicator exposed to fish spoilage (E2) confirms the interaction of H2S gas with the indicator (Fantauzzi et al., 2015; Tang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2023). The most important part of the XPS in the AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator is the part related to copper. The high-resolution spectrum of copper is divided into three separate peaks with binding energies at 933, 946, and 952 eV corresponding to Cu2p3/2, Cu2p3/2 sat (NIST database), and Cu2p1/2 in Cu+, respectively (Fig. 5F1) (Peng et al., 2020). Considering that the 2p3/2 combination between Cu(0) and Cu(I) can be close, the state of copper elements in the CuNPs/NCQDs complex was considered between zero and one valence (Huang et al., 2022). Xu et al. (2019) also reported the oxidation state of Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 at the binding energies of 933 and 952 eV as Cu+. As a result, these peaks are related to Cu2S (Han & Xu, 2012; Xu et al., 2019). Copper can react well with sulfur compounds because these peaks in the AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator got stronger after exposure to fish spoilage.

Selectivity is another critical requirement of a colorimetric indicator for accurate detection. Table 3 . shows that the prepared AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator did not show a significant color change to other gases produced during fish spoilage, confirming the high selectivity of this indicator to H2S gas.

One of the problems that may arise during the colorimetric identification of compounds is the effect of other compounds produced during fish spoilage. So, the interference effect of other gases during the detection of H2S gas by the prepared AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator was investigated. Table. 4 shows the colorimetric parameters of AH-CuNPs/NCQDs against different volatile compounds at different concentrations in the presence of H2S gas. There was no significant change in the colorimetric parameters of the indicators after exposure to other gases (p ≤ 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fish freshness monitoring using AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows the color changes of AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator during 10-day storage of fish from light green to dark yellow. Fig. 6 also shows that L* decreased, but b*, a*, and ΔE increased significantly during fish storage (p ≤ 0.05). The color changes of the indicator started on the second day of storage, and the changes were more intense in the last few days owing to the formation of CuS or Cu2S, which could be due to protein consumption by different species of Pseudomonas, Serratia, and Hafnia during the spoilage and production of H2S gas (Gram & Melchiorsen, 1996).

Fig. 7 shows the pH changes of trout fillet during storage at 4 °C. The initial pH of the fish was 6.17 ± 0.016. The pH increased with a slight slope during storage, reaching 6.78 ± 0.033 at the end of the storage period. Accumulation of volatile nitrogenous compounds (TMA, DMA, and ammonia) due to microbial activity, production of alkaline compounds such as indole, skatole, and mercaptan due to the activity of endogenous enzymes, and proteolysis that leads to alkalinization of the environment cause the pH increase during fish spoilage (Bouaziz et al., 2015; Kim et al., 2023; Susanto et al., 2011).

Biochemical characteristics such as TVB-N are indicators for measuring the quality of raw fish and indicate the presence of compounds such as ammonia, dimethylamine, and trimethylamine. These compounds are produced by bacterial activity and the action of endogenous enzymes (Goulas & Kontominas, 2007; Zhang et al., 2012). Fig. 7 shows the changes in TVB-N of the trout fillet during 10 days of storage at refrigerator temperature. The initial TVB-N value was 7.70 ± 0.98
mg
N
/
100
g
. TVB-N values increased with a gentle slope during storage and reached 40.6 ± 1.7
mg
N
/
100
g
at the end of storage. The fish is fresh if the TVB-N value is between 5 and 10, and it indicates the beginning of spoilage and complete spoilage of the fish, respectively, if the TVB-N reaches 25 and 30–35 (Kim et al., 2023; Nowzari et al., 2013). As a result, the trout fillet was fresh until the 5th day of storage. However, the fish began to spoil on the 6th day of storage and was corrupted entirely on the 8th. A slight increase in the content of TVB-N in the early stages of storage is related to the autolytic degradation of nucleotides and free amino acids, while a greater increase in the final stages of storage is related to microbial activity, autocatalytic activity, and the completion of the reduction reaction of TMAO to TMA (Sallam et al., 2007).

TVC is one of the most important indicators and tools for determining the freshness of aquatic products, which reflects changes in the number of spoilage microorganisms during storage (Li, Liu, et al., 2023; Lv et al., 2018). TVC represents a group of bacteria, including Pseudomonas, Aeromonas, H2S-producing bacteria, Enterobacteriaceae, and Lactic acid (Yu, Zhang, et al., 2018). TVC results of fish fillets during storage are shown in Fig. 7. TVC on the zero-day and the last day of storage were 4.15 ± 0.21 log (CFU/g) and 7.27 ± 0.3 log (CFU/g), respectively. Considering that if the total number of bacteria exceeds 7 log (CFU/g), it indicates fish spoilage and the production of dangerous metabolites for the consumer; therefore, the trout fillet was corrupted and unusable from the 8th day of storage, which was consistent with the results of TVB-N (He et al., 2020). According to the results of the investigation of the quality parameters of the fish, the trout fillet is fresh until the 5th day of storage in the refrigerator, but on the 6th day, it starts to spoil, and finally, on the 8th day, it is completely spoiled and cannot be consumed in any way. These states of the AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator color are green, pale yellow, and deep yellow, respectively.

A colorimetric freshness indicator was prepared based on agar hydrogel and CuNPs/NCQDs capable of sensitively and selectively detecting hydrogen sulfide gas during fish spoilage. The detection mechanism relied on the impact of the analyte on the nanoparticle surface, leading to the accumulation of nanoparticles and a subsequent change in their refractive index due to the formation of Cu-S/Cu2S. Consequently, the color of the indicator changed. Additionally, the CuNPs/NCQDs solution exhibited fluorescence properties. The fluorescence of CuNPs/NCQDs was turned off when it was exposed to hydrogen sulfide gas due to the internal filter effect mechanism. Notably, the prepared AH-CuNPs/NCQDs indicator was insensitive to pH changes and other volatile compounds. There was a strong correlation between the ΔE of the colorimetric indicator and quality parameters of trout fish. So, the indicator color changes increased with the increase in microbial load and fish spoilage. Overall, the AH-CuNPs/NCQDs colorimetric indicator offers advantages such as affordability, non-toxicity, sensitivity, real-time and with the naked eye detection. Future research directions may explore CuNPs/NCQDs migrating into food and potential toxicity, applicability to other perishable foods, and environmental implications.

Sepideh Lohrasbi Nejad: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hajar Shekarchizadeh: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.
